summary: In this chapter of a serialized fiction story, the narrator discovers their mother is missing and enlists their tech-savvy friend Dave to help investigate. They find that someone from a VPN in Atlanta has been changing passwords on family accounts. Using a commercial dashcam data marketplace, they track the mother's car movements through Manhattan and Brooklyn. Despite finding a reassuring note from the mother claiming she went to the Hamptons, the narrator remains suspicious about the circumstances and the mysterious 'skinner666' who accessed her accounts.
key_points: The story explores a near-future surveillance economy where anyone can pay to search millions of dashcams and CCTV feeds for license plates or other queries
Dave's hatred of police stems from childhood memories of attending BLM protests with his mother and later harassment by child protective services after a friend's suicide
The narrator observes that ubiquitous surveillance didn't end crime because 'ending crime was always more a question of will than a question of ability'
The dashcam marketplace model only uploads video when purchased, meaning offline devices create gaps in the surveillance net
The chapter builds tension between a mundane explanation (mom left a note) and sinister possibilities (hacked accounts, forced note)
money_quotes: Don't let the being high all the time fool you, he was the smartest guy in our friend group.
Ending crime was always more a question of will than a question of ability.
And most people never made back the money they spent on the camera, the data wasn't all that useful and not that many people bought it.
It seemed like there was a whole Internet that I didn't know about.
One government mooching pig was the same as the next, and none of them were on his side.
You're the true crime guy. I'm just the tech guy.
Was this just me being paranoid, or is that the exact note my mother would leave if she didn't want me to worry and that's not at all what happened?
key_insight: In a world of ubiquitous surveillance, the infrastructure exists to track anyone, but it's commodified, fragmented, and ultimately still leaves gaps that enable both privacy and crime.
